refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) does not exceed the customer-defined E_target over the agreed evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 as stated is a KPI support requirement but lacks specificity on measurement scope, boundary conditions, and operational context. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a measurable outcome dependent on operator behavior, vehicle efficiency, and system-level optimization. The requirement now explicitly bounds the evaluation period and scenario scope, making it verifiable and traceable to design decisions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish baseline energy consumption and ton-moved metrics via telemetry collection during controlled field trials. Compare measured energy_per_ton against E_target at agreed intervals. Verify that system design (DP) enables this outcome through efficiency-enhancing features (e.g., load optimization, route planning, idle reduction) and that no architectural choice (IM) introduces unnecessary energy overhead.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The system shall collect and report energy consumption data (kWh or fuel volume) with ≥95% accuracy from vehicle power systems.
FR-EE-002: The system shall collect and report work output (ton_moved) with ≥95% accuracy from load sensors and positioning systems.
FR-EE-003: The system shall provide real-time and post-session energy efficiency metrics (energy_per_ton) to operators and site managers.
FR-EE-004: The system shall support operator decision-making to minimize energy consumption per unit work (e.g., route suggestions, load optimization, idle alerts).
FR-EE-005: The system shall ensure that teleoperation, safety services, and all auxiliary systems do not introduce energy overhead exceeding 5% of baseline vehicle consumption.

candidate_dps: DP-EE-A: Implement energy monitoring and reporting subsystem with real-time telemetry feedback to operators; couple with vehicle efficiency optimization (load balancing, route planning) and operator coaching via HMI.

DP-EE-B: Implement centralized energy management controller that monitors vehicle power draw, predicts efficiency outcomes, and automatically adjusts operational parameters (throttle, speed, load distribution) to stay within E_target envelope.

DP-EE-C: Implement distributed energy monitoring across vehicle subsystems (drivetrain, hydraulics, auxiliary systems) with local optimization loops; aggregate data for system-level reporting and operator feedback.
DP-EE-D: Implement energy efficiency as a constraint in mission planning and task allocation; optimize task sequencing and vehicle routing to minimize total energy consumption across the worksite.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-EE-A + DP-EE-D (Integrated approach): Implement a two-tier energy efficiency strategy: (1) Real-time energy monitoring and operator feedback via telemetry and HMI to enable informed operator decisions and behavior change; (2) System-level task and route optimization to minimize energy consumption across the mission. This approach balances operator autonomy with system-level efficiency, is compatible with both manual and teleoperated modes, and distributes responsibility for efficiency across human and automated decision-making.

potential_sources: Energy monitoring (DP-EE-A) is independent of task optimization (DP-EE-D); they share data (energy metrics, task status) but do not create circular dependencies.
Both DPs depend on accurate telemetry collection (FR-EE-001, FR-EE-002), which is a lower-level concern and does not couple the DPs themselves.

Operator feedback loop (DP-EE-A) and automated optimization (DP-EE-D) may compete for control authority in edge cases (e.g., operator ignores efficiency suggestion); this is managed via clear priority rules and HMI design, not a fundamental coupling.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP avoids the tight coupling of DP-EE-B (automatic control) which would constrain operator autonomy and complicate safety certification. DP-EE-C (distributed optimization) is deferred to lower decomposition levels as an implementation detail of energy monitoring.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Operators will respond to real-time efficiency feedback and modify behavior to improve energy_per_ton. RISK: Operator compliance is variable; coaching and incentive structures may be needed.

ASSUMPTION: Task and route optimization algorithms can be computed within acceptable latency (< 5 sec) for dynamic worksite conditions. RISK: Optimization complexity may exceed real-time constraints; fallback heuristics required.

ASSUMPTION: Energy consumption data is available with sufficient accuracy and latency from vehicle power systems. RISK: Legacy vehicle interfaces may not provide real-time power telemetry; retrofitting or estimation required.

ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation and safety services do not introduce energy overhead exceeding 5% of baseline. RISK: Network communication, sensor polling, and safety monitoring may consume significant power; must be quantified and optimized.

RISK (High): Energy efficiency is sensitive to worksite conditions (terrain, load type, operator skill) that are outside system control. Evaluation period and scenario definition are critical to fair assessment.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-EE-A to explicitly specify the feedback mechanism (telemetry interval, HMI display format, alert thresholds) and DP-EE-D to define the optimization objective function (minimize total energy, minimize energy_per_ton, or minimize cost?). Clarify the decision authority boundary: when does the system recommend vs. mandate efficiency actions?

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules. Energy efficiency is a system-level property that emerges from the interaction of these modules. The NGHE Solution IM is responsible for: (1) defining and enforcing energy efficiency targets and evaluation criteria; (2) coordinating energy monitoring data collection across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules; (3) implementing system-level task and route optimization; (4) providing energy efficiency reporting and operator feedback via the HMI; (5) ensuring that auxiliary systems (Teleoperation, Safety Services) do not exceed energy overhead budgets.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Energy consumption data (kWh, fuel volume, power draw) flows from Vehicle to NGHE Solution; energy efficiency metrics and optimization recommendations flow from NGHE Solution to Vehicle and Teleoperation for operator display and control.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Mission tasks and work orders flow from site manager or planning system into NGHE Solution; task optimization logic uses this input to sequence and route tasks for energy efficiency.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services module provides safety constraints and alerts to NGHE Solution; NGHE Solution must ensure that energy optimization does not violate safety constraints (e.g., speed limits, load limits, no-go zones).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution uses Network Link to communicate with remote planning systems, site managers, and tele-operators for task distribution, telemetry reporting, and operator feedback.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural container for system-level energy efficiency strategy, task optimization, and cross-module coordination. Energy monitoring data collection is distributed across Vehicle and Teleoperation, but aggregation and reporting logic resides in NGHE Solution. Minimal diffusion: only the energy overhead budget constraint (FR-EE-005) may require implementation in Vehicle and Safety Services modules.

Vehicle module contains the physical energy sources (engine, battery) and consumption mechanisms (drivetrain, hydraulics, auxiliary systems). Energy monitoring sensors and local efficiency optimization (e.g., idle reduction, load balancing) are naturally contained in Vehicle. However, system-level task optimization and operator feedback are not Vehicle responsibilities; these belong in NGHE Solution. Diffusion risk: energy monitoring logic may be split between Vehicle (sensor data collection) and NGHE Solution (aggregation and reporting).

Teleoperation module is responsible for remote operator control and feedback. Energy efficiency feedback (real-time metrics, operator coaching) is a Teleoperation concern (HMI display, operator communication). However, the core energy monitoring and optimization logic should not reside in Teleoperation; this creates diffusion and couples Teleoperation to energy domain logic. Teleoperation should consume energy efficiency data from NGHE Solution and present it to the operator, not compute it.

Safety Services module is responsible for safety constraints and alerts. Energy efficiency optimization must respect safety constraints (e.g., no speed reduction below safe operating limits, no load redistribution that violates stability). However, Safety Services should not own energy efficiency logic; it should provide constraint inputs to NGHE Solution's optimization. Diffusion risk: if energy overhead budgets (FR-EE-005) are enforced in Safety Services, this creates tight coupling and diffusion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Energy efficiency as a system-level KPI is best owned by NGHE Solution, which has visibility into all modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and can coordinate optimization across them. NGHE Solution will implement: (1) energy monitoring data aggregation from Vehicle and Teleoperation; (2) task and route optimization logic; (3) operator feedback and coaching via HMI; (4) energy overhead budget enforcement. Vehicle will implement local energy monitoring sensors and report data via Telemetry interface. Teleoperation will display energy efficiency metrics to the operator and support operator decisions. Safety Services will provide safety constraints to NGHE Solution's optimization logic.
containment_risks: RISK: Energy monitoring data collection is distributed across Vehicle (sensors) and NGHE Solution (aggregation). If the interface between them is not well-defined, data quality and latency issues may arise. Mitigation: specify Telemetry interface contract (data types, update rates, accuracy requirements) at next decomposition level.

RISK: Task and route optimization logic in NGHE Solution may not have sufficient visibility into Vehicle capabilities and constraints (e.g., fuel consumption curves, load limits). Mitigation: Vehicle module must provide capability and constraint data to NGHE Solution via Telemetry or a dedicated interface.

RISK: Operator feedback loop (DP-EE-A) may conflict with automated optimization (DP-EE-D) if decision authority is not clearly defined. Mitigation: establish clear priority rules (e.g., operator can override system recommendations, but system logs and alerts on safety/efficiency impact).

RISK: Energy overhead budget constraint (FR-EE-005) may be difficult to enforce if Teleoperation and Safety Services modules are not designed with energy efficiency in mind. Mitigation: allocate energy budgets to each module at next decomposition level and track consumption via Telemetry.

RISK: Evaluation period and scenario definition (mentioned in refined FR) are critical to fair assessment but may not be fully specified at this level. Mitigation: establish evaluation protocol and baseline metrics as part of system requirements review.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the ownership boundary between NGHE Solution and Vehicle for energy monitoring: (1) Vehicle owns sensor data collection and local efficiency optimization (e.g., idle reduction); (2) NGHE Solution owns system-level energy monitoring, reporting, and task optimization. Define the Telemetry interface contract to specify energy data types, update rates, and accuracy requirements. Allocate energy overhead budgets to Teleoperation and Safety Services modules and define how these are tracked and enforced.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP (energy monitoring + task optimization) directly addresses the refined FR (achieve energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). The DP is decomposed into two complementary strategies (operator feedback and system optimization) that together enable the FR. No circular dependencies or missing logic detected. The DP is feasible within the constraints of the NGHE solution (manual and teleoperated modes, safety-critical environment).

diffusion_risks: Energy monitoring logic is distributed across Vehicle (sensor data collection) and NGHE Solution (aggregation and reporting). This is acceptable if the Telemetry interface is well-defined, but requires careful interface design to avoid data quality issues.

Task and route optimization logic in NGHE Solution depends on Vehicle capability data (fuel consumption curves, load limits). If this data is not available or is inconsistent, optimization quality will degrade. Mitigation: establish a Vehicle capability interface.

Energy overhead budget constraint (FR-EE-005) may require implementation in Vehicle and Safety Services modules. If not carefully managed, this could lead to diffusion of energy efficiency logic across multiple modules. Mitigation: define energy budget allocation and tracking at next decomposition level.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate container for system-level energy efficiency strategy. Vehicle and Teleoperation modules will implement supporting functions (energy monitoring, operator feedback) but will not own the core optimization logic. Safety Services will provide constraint inputs but will not own energy efficiency logic. This containment strategy minimizes diffusion and maintains clear separation of concerns.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: The refined FR is verifiable via field trials: measure energy_per_ton over the agreed evaluation period and compare against E_target. Verification requires: (1) accurate energy consumption data from Vehicle; (2) accurate work output data from load sensors; (3) clear definition of evaluation period and scenario scope. All of these are achievable with the selected IM (NGHE Solution) as the owner of energy efficiency KPI.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of energy efficiency responsibility is clear: NGHE Solution owns the KPI and system-level optimization; Vehicle owns energy monitoring sensors and local efficiency; Teleoperation owns operator feedback; Safety Services owns safety constraints. Each module has a well-defined role and interface. Verification of each module's contribution to the overall KPI is feasible via Telemetry data and performance metrics.

recommended_revisions: Define the Telemetry interface contract for energy data (types, update rates, accuracy, latency) at next decomposition level.
Establish a Vehicle capability interface to provide fuel consumption curves, load limits, and other efficiency-relevant parameters to NGHE Solution's optimization logic.
Allocate energy overhead budgets to Teleoperation and Safety Services modules and define how these are tracked and enforced via Telemetry.
Clarify the evaluation protocol for energy_per_ton: define the evaluation period, scenario scope, baseline conditions, and acceptance criteria.
Define the decision authority boundary for energy optimization: when does the system recommend vs. mandate efficiency actions? How does the operator override system recommendations?

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-formed and verifiable. The selected DP is feasible and addresses the FR without circular dependencies. The assigned IM (NGHE Solution) is appropriate and has acceptable containment scores. However, the DP and IM require further decomposition to address the sub-FRs (energy data collection, metrics reporting, operator feedback, system optimization, energy overhead budgets) and to define critical interfaces (Telemetry, Vehicle capability, energy budget tracking). The gate review identified several interface and allocation issues that must be resolved at the next decomposition level. Proceeding to decompose the sub-FRs will clarify these issues and enable detailed design.
decision.justification_detail: This is a ZIGZAG_DOWN decision because: (1) the current level (FR-DP-IM) is sound and passes all gates; (2) the sub-FRs are well-defined and ready for decomposition; (3) the next level will address critical interface and allocation details that are necessary for detailed design and verification; (4) no fundamental revisions to the current level are needed, but refinements will emerge from lower-level decomposition.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The system shall collect and report energy consumption data (kWh or fuel volume) with ≥95% accuracy from vehicle power systems.
FR-EE-002: The system shall collect and report work output (ton_moved) with ≥95% accuracy from load sensors and positioning systems.
FR-EE-003: The system shall provide real-time and post-session energy efficiency metrics (energy_per_ton) to operators and site managers.
FR-EE-004: The system shall support operator decision-making to minimize energy consumption per unit work (e.g., route suggestions, load optimization, idle alerts).
FR-EE-005: The system shall ensure that teleoperation, safety services, and all auxiliary systems do not introduce energy overhead exceeding 5% of baseline vehicle consumption.

assumptions: Operators will respond to real-time efficiency feedback and modify behavior to improve energy_per_ton.
Task and route optimization algorithms can be computed within acceptable latency (< 5 sec) for dynamic worksite conditions.
Energy consumption data is available with sufficient accuracy and latency from vehicle power systems.
Teleoperation and safety services do not introduce energy overhead exceeding 5% of baseline vehicle consumption.
Evaluation period and scenario definition are clearly specified and representative of customer worksite conditions.
Vehicle module can provide capability data (fuel consumption curves, load limits) to NGHE Solution's optimization logic.
Telemetry interface can support real-time energy data collection and reporting with acceptable latency and accuracy.
open_risks: RISK (High): Energy efficiency is sensitive to worksite conditions (terrain, load type, operator skill) that are outside system control. Evaluation period and scenario definition are critical to fair assessment.
RISK (High): Operator compliance with efficiency feedback is variable; coaching and incentive structures may be needed to achieve target energy_per_ton.
RISK (Medium): Task and route optimization complexity may exceed real-time constraints; fallback heuristics required.
RISK (Medium): Legacy vehicle interfaces may not provide real-time power telemetry; retrofitting or estimation required.
RISK (Medium): Energy overhead budget constraint (FR-EE-005) may be difficult to enforce if Teleoperation and Safety Services modules are not designed with energy efficiency in mind.
RISK (Medium): Operator feedback loop (DP-EE-A) may conflict with automated optimization (DP-EE-D) if decision authority is not clearly defined.
RISK (Low): Energy monitoring data collection is distributed across Vehicle and NGHE Solution; interface design must be careful to avoid data quality issues.
evidence_needed: Baseline energy consumption and work output metrics from field trials or historical data.
Customer-defined E_target value and evaluation period.
Vehicle power system interface specifications (data types, update rates, accuracy).
Load sensor and positioning system accuracy specifications.
Teleoperation and Safety Services energy consumption estimates.
Operator feedback and coaching effectiveness data (e.g., from pilot studies or similar systems).
Task and route optimization algorithm performance data (latency, solution quality).
Worksite scenario definitions and representative test cases for evaluation.
